input.prompt:
You are implementing Task 2: Trim Bootstrap SCSS to only the partials actually used

## Task Description

Read your task brief first: /Users/rod/build/begbie-hugo/.superpowers/sdd/task-2-brief.md
It contains the full task text from the plan, including the exact replacement import list.

## Context

This is a Hugo static site repo at /Users/rod/build/begbie-hugo (personal site for Rod
Begbie, using a vendored copy of the "portio" Hugo theme under themes/portio, with
Bootstrap 4.5.2's SCSS vendored under themes/portio/assets/bootstrap-4.5.2). This is Task
2 of a 3-task Phase 1 performance initiative. Task 1 (already done, committed) replaced
Font Awesome's icon webfont with inline SVG icons. This task is separate and independent:
`themes/portio/assets/scss/style.scss` currently imports Bootstrap's ENTIRE SCSS framework
via a single `@import "../bootstrap-4.5.2/scss/bootstrap"` line, even though a careful
survey of every class used across themes/portio/layouts found only grid, forms, buttons,
navbar, breadcrumb, pagination, and utility classes in actual use — no modals, tooltips,
popovers, dropdowns, carousels, cards, alerts, badges, etc. The task brief gives you the
exact curated replacement import list to swap in.

The repo's Hugo version locally is 0.163.3 (extended). Run `hugo` from the repo root to
build to public/ (gitignored). You can also run `hugo server -D` to view pages in a
browser if useful.

## Before You Begin

If you have questions about the requirements, the approach, or anything unclear in the
brief, ask them now.

## Your Job

1. Capture the baseline compiled CSS size before changing anything.
2. Make exactly the one edit specified in the brief (replace the single Bootstrap import
   line with the curated list of partials).
3. Rebuild. If Sass reports a missing variable/mixin error, the brief has specific
   guidance: identify which dropped partial defines the missing name by reading the
   relevant file under themes/portio/assets/bootstrap-4.5.2/scss/, and add just that
   partial back — do not guess, and do not add back partials "just in case."
4. Compare compiled CSS size before/after.
5. Visually spot-check with `hugo server -D` if you can (the controller will also do an
   independent visual pass later, so this is a sanity check, not the final word).

I diffed this list against upstream `themes/portio/assets/bootstrap-4.5.2/scss/bootstrap.scss` (which imports 27 partials in a fixed order) — the 15 kept partials are an exact ordered subset of that list, with `images, code, tables, dropdown, button-group, input-group, custom-forms, card, badge, jumbotron, alert, progress, media, list-group, close, toasts, modal, tooltip, popover, carousel, spinners, print` correctly dropped.

**No extra partials added back:** confirmed by diff stat (`1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)`) — no other partial lines appear anywhere in the file. The report's claim that the build compiled cleanly on the first attempt with no Sass errors, so nothing extra was needed, is consistent with the diff containing zero additional imports.

**No misapplication:** this is a straight line-for-line swap in place, at the same location in the file (right after `@import "variables";`, before `@import "typography";`), nothing else in the file touched.

⚠️ Cannot verify from diff alone (expected, non-diff-visible):
- Actual rendered visual equivalence across homepage/contact/blog-list/blog-single/mobile-navbar — the controller's independent visual pass should confirm this.
- The reported byte sizes (169,864 → 116,362) can't be verified from the diff, but I checked the current build artifact on disk without rebuilding: `public/scss/style.min.css` is currently 114 KB, consistent with the reported post-edit size.

### Strengths
- Import order exactly mirrors upstream Bootstrap's own dependency ordering (functions → variables → mixins → root → reboot → type → grid → forms → buttons → transitions → nav → navbar → breadcrumb → pagination → utilities) — this is the correct, safe way to do it, not an arbitrary reordering.
- Scope discipline: single file, single hunk, no unrelated SCSS touched.
- The report's selector-presence/absence check (confirming `.modal`, `.popover`, `.carousel`, `.card`, `.badge`, `.alert-` are gone, and that `.tooltip`/`.dropdown-menu` string hits are false positives from kept partials) shows real verification work rather than a rubber-stamp pass.
- The build succeeding at exit 0 with zero added-back partials is itself informative: it confirms none of the theme's own component SCSS (`components/resume-section.scss`, etc.) silently depends on a dropped Bootstrap mixin/variable — Sass would hard-error on that, not degrade silently.

None.

#### Important (Should Fix)
None.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)
None — the change is clean enough that I don't have nitpicks on the diff itself.

**On the blank-screenshot explanation:** I checked whether the diff could plausibly explain it. `resume__background` and the resume section's markup (`themes/portio/layouts/partials/resumeSection.html`) is styled by the theme's own `components/resume-section.scss` (imported separately, unaffected by this diff), and the only Bootstrap-derived classes involved (`tab-pane`, `tab-content`, `active`) live in `nav.scss`, which is on the kept list. Nothing in the trimmed import list touches this section. Combined with the implementer's `getComputedStyle` check (correct `background-color`, `opacity: 1`, `display: block`) and a subsequent successful full-page screenshot of the same section, the "capture-timing artifact, not a CSS regression" explanation is credible and consistent with what the diff shows — it is not a case of the diff plausibly having caused it.

**Task quality:** Approved
**Reasoning:** The import-list swap exactly matches the brief, preserves correct Bootstrap-internal dependency order, touches no unrelated code, and the one anomaly flagged (blank screenshot) traces to a theme-owned SCSS component and a kept Bootstrap partial, not to anything the diff removed — a credible non-regression explanation, not a hand-wave.</result>

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/rod/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers-chrome/3.0.2/skills/browsing

# Browsing with Chrome Direct

## Overview

Control Chrome via DevTools Protocol using the `use_browser` MCP tool. Single unified interface with auto-starting Chrome.

**Announce:** "I'm using the browsing skill to control Chrome."

## When to Use

**Use this when:**
- Controlling authenticated sessions
- Managing multiple tabs in running browser
- Playwright MCP unavailable or excessive

**Use Playwright MCP when:**
- Need fresh browser instances
- Generating screenshots/PDFs
- Prefer higher-level abstractions

## Auto-Capture

Every DOM action (navigate, click, type, select, eval, keyboard_press, hover, drag_drop, double_click, right_click, file_upload) automatically saves:
- `{prefix}.png` — viewport screenshot
- `{prefix}.md` — page content as structured markdown
- `{prefix}.html` — full rendered DOM
- `{prefix}-console.txt` — browser console messages

Files are saved to the session directory with sequential prefixes (001-navigate, 002-click, etc.). You must check these before using extract or screenshot actions.

## The use_browser Tool

Single MCP tool with action-based interface. Chrome auto-starts on first use.

**Parameters:**
- `action` (required): Operation to perform
- `selector` (optional): CSS or XPath selector for element operations
- `payload` (optional): Action-specific data (string or object)
- `timeout` (optional): Timeout in ms for await operations (default: 5000)

**Active tab**: Every action operates on the current `activeTab`. Use `switch_tab` to change it.

## Actions Reference

### Navigation
- **navigate**: Navigate to URL
  - `payload`: URL string
  - Example: `{action: "navigate", payload: "https://example.com"}`

- **await_element**: Wait for element to appear
  - `selector`: CSS selector
  - `timeout`: Max wait time in ms
  - Example: `{action: "await_element", selector: ".loaded", timeout: 10000}`

- **await_text**: Wait for text to appear
  - `payload`: Text to wait for
  - Example: `{action: "await_text", payload: "Welcome"}`

### Interaction
- **click**: Click element
  - `selector`: CSS selector
  - Example: `{action: "click", selector: "button.submit"}`

- **type**: Text input
  - `selector`: Optional — clicks to focus first
  - `payload`: Text to type (`\t`=Tab, `\n`=Enter)
  - Example: `{action: "type", selector: "#email", payload: "user@example.com"}`

- **double_click**: Double-click element (fires dblclick event)
  - `selector`: CSS selector
  - Example: `{action: "double_click", selector: ".item"}`

- **right_click**: Right-click element (fires contextmenu event)
  - `selector`: CSS selector
  - Example: `{action: "right_click", selector: ".row"}`

- **select**: Select dropdown option
  - `selector`: CSS selector
  - `payload`: Option value(s)
  - Example: `{action: "select", selector: "select[name=state]", payload: "CA"}`

- **keyboard_press**: Press special keys (Tab, Enter, Escape, Arrow keys, F1-F12)
  - `payload`: Key name (string) or `{"key": "Tab", "modifiers": {"shift": true, "ctrl": false, "alt": false, "meta": false}}`
  - Example: `{action: "keyboard_press", payload: "Tab"}`
  - Example with modifiers: `{action: "keyboard_press", payload: {"key": "Tab", "modifiers": {"shift": true}}}`

### Mouse Actions (CDP-Level)
These use CDP Input.dispatchMouseEvent, bypassing synthetic event restrictions.

- **hover**: Move mouse over element (CSS :hover, tooltips, menus)
  - `selector`: CSS selector
  - Example: `{action: "hover", selector: ".menu-trigger"}`

- **drag_drop**: Drag element to target (native drag-and-drop via CDP)
  - `selector`: Source element
  - `payload`: Target selector or JSON coordinates `{"x":N,"y":N}`
  - Example: `{action: "drag_drop", selector: ".card", payload: ".column-2"}`

- **mouse_move**: Move mouse to coordinates
  - `payload`: JSON `{"x":N,"y":N}` (optional: `steps`, `fromX`, `fromY` for smooth movement)
  - Example: `{action: "mouse_move", payload: "{\"x\":100,\"y\":200}"}`

- **scroll**: Scroll via mouse wheel events
  - `payload`: Direction (up/down/left/right) or JSON `{"deltaX":N,"deltaY":N}`
  - `selector`: Optional — scroll within element
  - Example: `{action: "scroll", payload: "down"}`

### File Upload
- **file_upload**: Set files on input[type=file] elements (can't be done via JavaScript)
  - `selector`: File input element
  - `payload`: File path or JSON `{"files":["/path/a.pdf","/path/b.jpg"]}`
  - Example: `{action: "file_upload", selector: "#upload", payload: "/tmp/doc.pdf"}`

### Extraction
- **extract**: Get page content
  - `payload`: Format ('markdown'|'text'|'html')
  - `selector`: Optional - limit to element
  - Example: `{action: "extract", payload: "markdown"}`
  - Example: `{action: "extract", payload: "text", selector: "h1"}`

- **attr**: Get element attribute
  - `selector`: CSS selector
  - `payload`: Attribute name
  - Example: `{action: "attr", selector: "a.download", payload: "href"}`

- **eval**: Execute JavaScript
  - `payload`: JavaScript code
  - Example: `{action: "eval", payload: "document.title"}`

### Export
- **screenshot**: Capture screenshot of a specific element
  - `payload`: Filename
  - `selector`: Optional - screenshot specific element
  - Viewport screenshots are auto-captured after every DOM action. Use this only when you need a specific element.
  - Example: `{action: "screenshot", payload: "/tmp/chart.png", selector: ".chart"}`

### Tab Management
- **list_tabs**: List all open tabs
  - Example: `{action: "list_tabs"}`

- **new_tab**: Create new tab
  - Example: `{action: "new_tab"}`

- **close_tab**: Close the active tab
  - Example: `{action: "close_tab"}`

- **switch_tab**: Switch the active tab (sticky — stays until changed)
  - `payload`: Tab index (number), URL substring, or title substring
  - Example: `{action: "switch_tab", payload: 1}` (by index)
  - Example: `{action: "switch_tab", payload: "example.com"}` (by URL substring)
  - Example: `{action: "switch_tab", payload: "GitHub"}` (by title substring)

### Browser Mode Control
- **show_browser**: Make browser window visible (headed mode)
  - Example: `{action: "show_browser"}`
  - ⚠️ **WARNING**: Restarts Chrome, reloads pages via GET, loses POST state

- **hide_browser**: Switch to headless mode (invisible browser)
  - Example: `{action: "hide_browser"}`
  - ⚠️ **WARNING**: Restarts Chrome, reloads pages via GET, loses POST state

- **browser_mode**: Check current browser mode, port, and profile
  - Example: `{action: "browser_mode"}`
  - Returns: `{"headless": true|false, "mode": "headless"|"headed", "running": true|false, "port": 9222, "profile": "name", "profileDir": "/path"}`

### Profile Management
- **set_profile**: Change Chrome profile (must kill Chrome first)
  - Example: `{action: "set_profile", "payload": "browser-user"}`
  - ⚠️ **WARNING**: Chrome must be stopped first
  - **Side effect**: marks the profile as explicit, opting out of auto-disambiguation (see below)

- **get_profile**: Get current profile name and directory
  - Example: `{action: "get_profile"}`
  - Returns: `{"profile": "name", "profileDir": "/path"}`

**Default behavior**: Chrome starts in **headless mode** with **"superpowers-chrome" profile** on a **dynamically allocated port** (range 9222-12111). Override the port with `CHROME_WS_PORT`; override the profile with `CHROME_WS_PROFILE`.

**Auto-disambiguation across parallel MCPs**:
When two MCP servers start on the same host with the default profile, the first claims `superpowers-chrome` (port 9222) and later ones silently fall through to `superpowers-chrome-2` (port 9223), `superpowers-chrome-3`, etc. Each MCP drives its own Chrome with its own profile dir; they don't fight over `activeTab`. The bridge tracks ownership via a lock file at `~/.cache/superpowers/browser-profiles/<profile>.mcp.lock`; stale locks (dead PIDs) are reclaimed automatically.

To opt **out** of disambiguation — e.g., to intentionally share Chrome between a long-lived `chrome-ws` CLI session and your MCP — set the profile name explicitly:
- Env var: `REDACTED`
- Or: `{action: "set_profile", payload: "my-profile"}` at runtime

An explicit profile name still acquires the lock, but on conflict the bridge **shares** rather than disambiguates — the second process reconnects to the first's Chrome (the original reconnect-on-restart behavior).

### Chrome Lifecycle (Recovery)
- **kill_chrome**: Kill the Chrome process this MCP is driving
  - Example: `{action: "kill_chrome"}`
  - Releases the meta.json; next page action auto-restarts Chrome

- **restart_chrome**: kill_chrome + immediate spawn
  - Example: `{action: "restart_chrome"}`

**Auto-restart banner**: when the bridge has to spawn a fresh Chrome (because the previous one died or was killed externally — e.g., `kill -9 <pid>` from the shell), the first response after the restart prepends:
```
[Chrome auto-restarted; URL reset to about:blank. Re-navigate to continue.]
```
Treat this as a signal that your prior URL / tab state is gone — re-navigate before assuming anything about the current page.

### Console Logging
Capture browser console output for the active tab. Buffer is keyed by the page session's `sessionId`, so it survives `close_tab`/`new_tab` ordering quirks. Levels: `log`, `info`, `warn`, `error`.

- **enable_console_logging**: Start capturing
  - Example: `{action: "enable_console_logging"}`

- **get_console_messages**: Read captured messages
  - All: `{action: "get_console_messages"}`
  - Since timestamp (epoch ms): `{action: "get_console_messages", payload: {since: 1716000000000}}`
  - Returns: array of `{timestamp, level, text}` entries

- **clear_console_messages**: Reset the buffer
  - Example: `{action: "clear_console_messages"}`

### Dialog Handling
Native dialogs (JS alert/confirm/prompt, beforeunload, HTTP basic-auth, permission prompts, device choosers) pause the page. While a dialog is open, page-targeted actions (`extract`, `click`, `eval`, etc.) return a refusal whose text contains `Page is behind a dialog` and lists the available `dialog::*` selectors.

When a dialog fires **during** a `navigate` (typical for HTTP basic-auth), `navigate` itself throws with the dialog grammar in the message — you don't have to issue a separate page-targeted call to discover the dialog.

Handle dialogs by clicking/typing a `dialog::*` selector:
- `{action: "click", selector: "dialog::accept"}` — accept JS alert/confirm/prompt, beforeunload, permission grant
- `{action: "click", selector: "dialog::dismiss"}` — dismiss / cancel / deny
- `{action: "type", selector: "dialog::prompt", payload: "text"}` then accept — respond to JS prompt
- `{action: "type", selector: "dialog::username", payload: "alice"}` + `{action: "type", selector: "dialog::password", payload: "secret"}` + `{action: "click", selector: "dialog::accept"}` — HTTP basic-auth
- `{action: "click", selector: "dialog::device[id=\"<deviceId>\"]"}` — pick a WebUSB/Bluetooth/Serial/HID device

**Critical caveats when toggling modes**:
1. **Chrome must restart** - Cannot switch headless/headed mode on running Chrome
2. **Pages reload via GET** - All open tabs are reopened with GET requests
3. **POST state is lost** - Form submissions, POST results, and POST-based navigation will be lost
4. **Session state is lost** - Any client-side state (JavaScript variables, etc.) is cleared
5. **Cookies/auth may persist** - Uses same user data directory, so logged-in sessions may survive

**When to use headed mode**:
- Debugging visual rendering issues
- Demonstrating browser behavior to user
- Testing features that only work with visible browser
- Debugging issues that don't reproduce in headless mode

**When to stay in headless mode** (default):
- All other cases - faster, cleaner, less intrusive
- Screenshots work perfectly in headless mode
- Most automation works identically in both modes

**Profile management**:
Profiles store persistent browser data (cookies, localStorage, extensions, auth sessions).

**Profile locations**:
- macOS: `~/Library/Caches/superpowers/browser-profiles/{name}/`
- Linux: `~/.cache/superpowers/browser-profiles/{name}/`
- Windows: `%LOCALAPPDATA%/superpowers/browser-profiles/{name}/`

**When to use separate profiles**:
- **Default profile ("superpowers-chrome")**: General automation, shared sessions
- **Agent-specific profiles**: Isolate different agents' browser state
  - Example: browser-user agent uses "browser-user" profile
- **Task-specific profiles**: Testing with different user contexts
  - Example: "test-logged-in" vs "test-logged-out"

**Profile data persists across**:
- Chrome restarts
- Mode toggles (headless ↔ headed)
- System reboots (data is in cache directory)

**To use a different profile**:
1. Kill Chrome if running: `await chromeLib.killChrome()`
2. Set profile: `{action: "set_profile", "payload": "my-profile"}`
3. Start Chrome: Next navigate/action will use new profile

## Quick Start Pattern

```
Navigate and extract:
{action: "navigate", payload: "https://example.com"}
{action: "await_element", selector: "h1"}
{action: "extract", payload: "text", selector: "h1"}
```

## Common Patterns

### Fill and Submit Form
```
{action: "navigate", payload: "https://example.com/login"}
{action: "await_element", selector: "input[name=email]"}
{action: "type", selector: "input[name=email]", payload: "user@example.com"}
{action: "type", selector: "input[name=password]", payload: "pass123"}
{action: "keyboard_press", payload: "Enter"}
{action: "await_text", payload: "Welcome"}
```

Uses `keyboard_press` to submit the form.

### Multi-Tab Workflow
```
{action: "list_tabs"}
{action: "switch_tab", payload: 2}
{action: "click", selector: "a.email"}
{action: "await_element", selector: ".content"}
{action: "extract", payload: "text", selector: ".amount"}
```

### Dynamic Content
```
{action: "navigate", payload: "https://example.com"}
{action: "type", selector: "input[name=q]", payload: "query"}
{action: "click", selector: "button.search"}
{action: "await_element", selector: ".results"}
{action: "extract", payload: "text", selector: ".result-title"}
```

### Get Link Attribute
```
{action: "navigate", payload: "https://example.com"}
{action: "await_element", selector: "a.download"}
{action: "attr", selector: "a.download", payload: "href"}
```

### Execute JavaScript
```
{action: "eval", payload: "document.querySelectorAll('a').length"}
{action: "eval", payload: "Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('a')).map(a => a.href)"}
```

### Resize Viewport (Responsive Testing)
Use `eval` to resize the browser window for testing responsive layouts:
```
{action: "eval", payload: "window.resizeTo(375, 812); 'Resized to mobile'"}
{action: "eval", payload: "window.resizeTo(768, 1024); 'Resized to tablet'"}
{action: "eval", payload: "window.resizeTo(1920, 1080); 'Resized to desktop'"}
```

**Note**: This resizes the window, not device emulation. It won't change:
- Device pixel ratio (retina displays)
- Touch events
- User-Agent string

For most responsive testing, window resize is sufficient.

### Clear Cookies
Use `eval` to clear cookies accessible to JavaScript:
```
{action: "eval", payload: "document.cookie.split(';').forEach(c => { document.cookie = c.trim().split('=')[0] + '=;expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT;path=/'; }); 'Cookies cleared'"}
```

**Note**: This clears cookies accessible to JavaScript. It won't clear:
- httpOnly cookies (server-side only)
- Cookies from other domains

For most logout/reset scenarios, this is sufficient.

### Scroll Page
```
{action: "scroll", payload: "down"}
{action: "scroll", payload: "up"}
{action: "scroll", selector: ".container", payload: "{\"deltaX\":0,\"deltaY\":500}"}
```

Uses real mouse wheel events (vs `eval` + `scrollTo` which bot detectors flag).

## Tips

**Always wait before interaction:**
Don't click or fill immediately after navigate - pages need time to load.

```
// BAD - might fail if page slow
{action: "navigate", payload: "https://example.com"}
{action: "click", selector: "button"}  // May fail!

// GOOD - wait first
{action: "navigate", payload: "https://example.com"}
{action: "await_element", selector: "button"}
{action: "click", selector: "button"}
```

**Use specific selectors:**
Avoid generic selectors that match multiple elements.

```
// BAD - matches first button
{action: "click", selector: "button"}

// GOOD - specific
{action: "click", selector: "button[type=submit]"}
{action: "click", selector: "#login-button"}
```

**Submit forms:**
Use `keyboard_press` with Enter after `type`, or append `\n` to the payload.

```
{action: "type", selector: "#search", payload: "query"}
{action: "keyboard_press", payload: "Enter"}
```

**Check content first:**
Extract page content to verify selectors before building workflow.

```
{action: "extract", payload: "html"}
```

## Troubleshooting

**Element not found:**
- Use `await_element` before interaction
- Verify selector with `extract` action using 'html' format

**Timeout errors:**
- Increase timeout: `{timeout: 30000}` for slow pages
- Wait for specific element instead of text

**Wrong tab active:**
- Use `list_tabs` to see all open tabs
- Use `switch_tab` with a URL or title substring to reliably switch tabs
- Tab indices shift when tabs close — prefer URL/title-based switching

**eval returns `[object Object]`:**
- Use `JSON.stringify()` for complex objects: `{action: "eval", payload: "JSON.stringify({name: 'test'})"}`
- For async functions: `{action: "eval", payload: "JSON.stringify(await yourAsyncFunction())"}`

## Test Automation (Advanced)

<details>
<summary>Click to expand test automation guidance</summary>

When building test automation, you have two approaches:

### Approach 1: use_browser MCP (Simple Tests)
Best for: Single-step tests, direct Claude control during conversation

```json
{"action": "navigate", "payload": "https://app.com"}
{"action": "click", "selector": "#test-button"}
{"action": "eval", "payload": "JSON.stringify({passed: document.querySelector('.success') !== null})"}
```

### Approach 2: chrome-ws CLI (Complex Tests)
Best for: Multi-step test suites, standalone automation scripts

**Key insight**: `chrome-ws` is the reference implementation showing proper Chrome DevTools Protocol usage. When `use_browser` doesn't work as expected, examine how `chrome-ws` handles the same operation.

```bash
# Example: Automated form testing
./chrome-ws navigate 0 "https://app.com/form"
./chrome-ws fill 0 "#email" "test@example.com"
./chrome-ws click 0 "button[type=submit]"
./chrome-ws wait-text 0 "Success"
```

### When use_browser Fails
1. **Check chrome-ws source code** - It shows the correct CDP pattern
2. **Use chrome-ws to verify** - Test the same operation via CLI
3. **Adapt the pattern** - Apply the working CDP approach to use_browser

### Common Test Automation Patterns
- **Form validation**: Fill forms, check error states
- **UI state testing**: Click elements, verify DOM changes
- **Performance testing**: Measure load times, capture metrics
- **Screenshot comparison**: Capture before/after states

</details>

## Advanced Usage

For command-line usage outside Claude Code, see [COMMANDLINE-USAGE.md](COMMANDLINE-USAGE.md).

For detailed examples, see [EXAMPLES.md](EXAMPLES.md).

## Protocol Reference

Full CDP documentation: https://chromedevtools.github.io/devtools-protocol/

input.prompt:
You are a Senior Code Reviewer with expertise in software architecture,
design patterns, and best practices. Your job is to review completed work
against its plan or requirements and identify issues before they cascade.
This is the final whole-branch review before this branch is merged/PR'd.

## What Was Implemented

Phase 1 of a 3-phase performance initiative on a personal Hugo static site
(Rod Begbie's site, themes/portio vendored Bootstrap 4.5.2-based theme).
This phase cuts the two biggest CSS/asset payload contributors:

1. Replaced Font Awesome's full icon webfont (1.1MB for 6 distinct icons
   actually used) with a new Hugo partial (`icon.html`) that renders
   inline SVG icons, using exact path data fetched from Font Awesome's
   official GitHub repo (7.3.0, free/CC-BY-4.0 icons).
2. Trimmed `style.scss`'s Bootstrap SCSS import from the entire framework
   (~35 partials) down to only the 15 partials the theme's templates
   actually use (grid, forms, buttons, navbar, breadcrumb, pagination,
   utilities, plus foundation partials functions/variables/mixins/root/
   reboot/type/transitions).

Each of the 2 implementation tasks was independently reviewed (spec
compliance + code quality) and approved before merge into this branch. A
third, verification-only task confirmed: no Font Awesome or
dropped-Bootstrap-component references remain, a clean `hugo` build,
measured payload reduction (style.min.css 169,864 -> 116,362 bytes,
~31.5% smaller; font-awesome directory fully removed;
public/plugins 1.8MB -> 692KB), and a visual pass via Chrome DevTools
across the homepage, mobile nav toggle, contact page, blog list, and a
blog single post — all rendering correctly with no visual regressions.

One known, accepted quirk found during verification: `skillSection.html`
has `class="progress"`/`class="progress-bar"` which superficially matches
a "dropped Bootstrap component" grep pattern, but is actually styled by
the theme's own `_skill-section.scss` (not Bootstrap's dropped
`_progress.scss`) — and the skill section is disabled in
`data/skillSection.yml` (`enable: false`) anyway, so this is a non-issue,
not a regression. Please independently sanity-check this reasoning rather
than taking it on faith.

## Requirements / Plan

Read the design spec:
docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-30-css-modernization-phase1-design.md
Read the implementation plan:
docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-30-css-modernization-phase1.md

Global constraints:
- No visual change to any page (icons must look identical in size/color/
  position; grid/spacing/nav/buttons/forms/breadcrumbs/pagination must
  render identically after the Bootstrap trim).
- Icon SVG path data must be the exact literal Font Awesome path data
  (not regenerated/approximated).
- Scope is exactly: themes/portio/layouts/partials/icon.html (new),
  themes/portio/layouts/partials/testimonialSection.html,
  themes/portio/layouts/partials/footer.html,
  themes/portio/layouts/portfolio/single.html,
  themes/portio/layouts/partials/head.html,
  themes/portio/assets/scss/_common.scss,
  themes/portio/assets/scss/components/_testimonial-section.scss,
  themes/portio/assets/scss/style.scss, and deleting
  themes/portio/static/plugins/font-awesome/. Nothing else (JS, other
  SCSS components, data/*.yml, content/*.md, config.toml) should change
  in this phase — that's Phase 2 (JS) and Phase 3 (asset pipeline),
  separate specs not yet started.

## Git Range to Review

**Base:** 343700b415aa69ac7e0f0aebafa607c840065b77 (merge-base with main)
**Head:** HEAD (branch chore/css-modernize-phase1)

A pre-generated review package is at:
/Users/rod/build/begbie-hugo/.superpowers/sdd/review-final-phase1.diff

This contains the commit log, the full `git diff --stat` (including the
font-awesome directory deletion — ~15 files, ~5000 lines of deleted
vendor CSS/font content), followed by the full diff body for every file
EXCEPT the font-awesome directory (whose deletion is fully captured by
the stat — no need to review deleted vendor content line-by-line). Read
this file once — it is your primary view of the change.

If useful, you may run narrow, targeted checks beyond the diff (e.g.
`git show HEAD:themes/portio/assets/scss/components/_skill-section.scss`
to verify the progress-bar reasoning above, or checking
`data/skillSection.yml` for the `enable: false` claim) — but don't
re-derive the whole diff with git commands, and don't crawl the broader
codebase without a specific, named reason.

## Read-Only Review

Your review is read-only on this checkout. Do not mutate the working
tree, the index, HEAD, or branch state in any way.

## What to Check

**Plan alignment:**
- Does the implementation match the plan across both tasks?
- Are there any deviations from the plan? If so, flag them specifically.
- Is all planned functionality present (all 7 icons, all 4 call sites
  migrated, both SCSS changes, Font Awesome plugin removed, Bootstrap
  import trimmed to exactly the specified 15 partials)?
- Is anything outside the declared scope touched?

**Code quality:**
- Clean, consistent icon.html implementation?
- Any leftover Font Awesome or dropped-Bootstrap-component references?
- DRY without premature abstraction?

**Architecture:**
- Does this integrate cleanly with the rest of the theme?
- Any security concerns (e.g., the `safeHTML` use in icon.html — is it
  scoped safely, i.e. only rendering fixed literal SVG strings from the
  partial's own dict, never user-controlled input)?

**Testing:**
- The per-task reports/reviews already covered build success and visual
  spot-checks. Sanity-check that the claimed payload numbers are
  consistent with what's in the diff (e.g., does the stat match the
  claimed byte reduction direction and magnitude?).

**Production readiness:**
- No schema/data changes involved. Documentation: do the spec and plan
  docs in this branch accurately reflect what was actually implemented?

## Calibration

Categorize issues by actual severity. Not everything is Critical.
Acknowledge what was done well before listing issues. If you find
significant deviations from the plan, flag them specifically so they can
be confirmed as intentional or not. If you find issues with the plan/spec
itself rather than the implementation, say so.

## Output Format

### Strengths

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)
#### Important (Should Fix)
#### Minor (Nice to Have)

For each issue: file:line reference, what's wrong, why it matters, how to fix.

### Recommendations

### Assessment

**Ready to merge?** [Yes | No | With fixes]

**Reasoning:** [1-2 sentence technical assessment]

## Critical Rules

DO: categorize by actual severity, be specific (file:line), explain WHY each issue
matters, acknowledge strengths, give a clear verdict.

DON'T: say "looks good" without checking, mark nitpicks as Critical, give feedback on
code you didn't actually read, be vague, avoid giving a clear verdict.

<result>Review complete. I read the full diff package, both spec and plan docs, and independently verified the skill-section reasoning, the `enable: false` claim, the on-disk `icon.html`, and swept for residual references.

---

### Strengths

- **Scope is exactly honoured.** The `git diff --stat` contains precisely the 8 declared source files + the font-awesome directory deletion + the 2 planning docs. Nothing in `data/*.yml`, `content/*.md`, `config.toml`, JS, or other SCSS components was touched. Phase boundaries respected.
- **Clean, DRY icon partial.** A single `dict`-driven partial with an optional `class` param replaces 4 call sites without over-engineering. The `{{ with $class }} {{ . }}{{ end }}` guard avoids the stray trailing space the spec's earlier draft (`icon--{{ .name }} {{ $class }}`) would have produced — a genuine improvement over the spec.
- **`safeHTML` is safely scoped.** `index $icons .name | safeHTML` only ever renders fixed literal SVG strings from the partial's own dict. `.name` (site-owner-controlled config or hardcoded literals) selects a key; a miss returns nil and renders empty. No user-controlled input ever reaches `safeHTML`. No injection surface.
- **Correct SCSS selector migration.** `_testimonial-section.scss` `i` → `.icon` compiles to `.icon` / `.icon.inactive`, and the rendered span carries `class="icon icon--fa-star inactive"`, so both colour rules still match. `fill="currentColor"` + the new `.icon` colour rule preserves the colouring mechanism.
- **No residual references.** Zero `<i>` tags, zero `fa fa-`/`class="fa `/`font-awesome` references remain in layouts, and nothing outside the deleted dir/docs references the removed CSS path. `head.html` `<link>` cleanly removed.
- **The skill-section "non-issue" reasoning holds up.** I confirmed independently: `_skill-section.scss` defines its own `.progress`/`.progress-bar` rules (width, background, height, border-radius, animation) under `.skill__progress_item.js-animation` — fully self-contained, not dependent on Bootstrap's dropped `_progress.scss`. And `data/skillSection.yml` line 2 is `enable: false`, so the section never renders anyway. Not a regression.
- **Payload numbers are directionally consistent.** ~5,000 deleted lines of vendor CSS + 5 removed binary webfont files matches the ~1.1MB font-awesome removal; dropping ~20 Bootstrap partials is consistent with the claimed ~31.5% `style.min.css` reduction.

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)
None.

#### Important (Should Fix)
None.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)

1. **`.icon svg { width: 1em; height: 1em }` ignores non-square viewBoxes** — `themes/portio/assets/scss/_common.scss:14-15`. Forcing both dimensions to `1em` squashes icons whose viewBox isn't square: the brand squares are `448×512` (rendered ~14% wider than tall) and `fa-star` is `576×512`. Font Awesome's own renderer preserves aspect ratio (`height:1em; width:<aspect>em`). The visual pass reportedly found no regression, so this is acceptable, but the more faithful fix is `svg { height: 1em; width: auto }` (or per-icon aspect). Worth noting because a future icon with a more extreme aspect (e.g. the `384×512` map-marker) would visibly distort.

2. **Orphaned `.fa-ul` / `.fa-li` classes in footer** — `themes/portio/layouts/partials/footer.html:66-70`. The address widget still uses `class="fa-ul"` / `class="fa-li"`, which were styled only by the now-deleted font-awesome CSS. No current regression because the entire block sits inside an HTML comment (closing `</div> -->` at line 74, confirmed). But it's a latent trap: if anyone uncomments the address widget later, the list layout will be unstyled. Out of this phase's stated icon-line scope, so leaving it is defensible — just flagging the loose end.

3. **Spec doc's icon snippet is stale vs the implementation** — `docs/.../2026-06-30-css-modernization-phase1-design.md:104-114` shows escaped double-quote strings with inline `width="1em" height="1em"` on the SVG, whereas the shipped partial uses backtick raw strings with **no** inline sizing (sizing moved to CSS). The *plan* doc (lines 67-79) matches the implementation exactly and is clearly the authoritative artifact; the spec snippet is illustrative (`"..."`). Minor accuracy drift only.

4. **Provenance of `fa-twitter-square` / `fa-facebook-official` path data is unverified here** — these FA4-era brand names don't exist in Font Awesome 7.3.0 (twitter-square became x-twitter; facebook-official was retired), yet the spec cites 7.3.0 as the source. The embedded paths do render the correct classic glyphs (bird-in-square, f-in-square), but I could not independently confirm the literal bytes are unmodified FA data without fetching upstream, which was out of my read-only remit. This rests on the prior per-task review. Low priority — the icons are only reachable via the unused testimonial/social paths and the dead portfolio template.

5. **Process: planning artifacts are committed on the branch** — the plan and spec under `docs/superpowers/` are part of this diff and will merge to `main`. Your own workflow convention is "don't merge planning artifacts to main." The review brief explicitly includes them, so this may be intentional for this personal repo — just confirming it's a deliberate choice, not an oversight.

### Recommendations

- If you want belt-and-braces icon fidelity, switch `_common.scss` to `svg { height: 1em; width: auto }` and re-run the visual pass on the star rating + footer socials. Optional; current output passed inspection.
- Consider a Phase 2 (or a quick follow-up) note to strip the orphaned `.fa-ul`/`.fa-li` from the commented address widget so it doesn't mislead later.
- Decide whether the `docs/superpowers/` artifacts should land on `main` or be kept off it per your usual convention.

### Assessment

**Ready to merge?** Yes

**Reasoning:** The implementation matches the plan step-for-step, stays exactly within declared scope, introduces no security or correctness issues, and the one "known quirk" (skill-section progress classes) is independently confirmed to be a genuine non-issue. All findings are Minor polish/process items, none blocking.</result>

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/rod/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/6.1.0/skills/finishing-a-development-branch

# Finishing a Development Branch

## Overview

Guide completion of development work by presenting clear options and handling chosen workflow.

**Core principle:** Verify tests → Detect environment → Present options → Execute choice → Clean up.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."

## The Process

### Step 1: Verify Tests

**Before presenting options, verify tests pass:**

```bash
# Run project's test suite
npm test / cargo test / pytest / go test ./...
```

**If tests fail:**
```
Tests failing (<N> failures). Must fix before completing:

[Show failures]

Cannot proceed with merge/PR until tests pass.
```

Stop. Don't proceed to Step 2.

**If tests pass:** Continue to Step 2.

### Step 2: Detect Environment

**Determine workspace state before presenting options:**

```bash
GIT_DIR=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
GIT_COMMON=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
```

This determines which menu to show and how cleanup works:

| State | Menu | Cleanup |
|-------|------|---------|
| `GIT_DIR == GIT_COMMON` (normal repo) | Standard 4 options | No worktree to clean up |
| `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON`, named branch | Standard 4 options | Provenance-based (see Step 6) |
| `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON`, detached HEAD | Reduced 3 options (no merge) | No cleanup (externally managed) |

### Step 3: Determine Base Branch

```bash
# Try common base branches
git merge-base HEAD main 2>/dev/null || git merge-base HEAD master 2>/dev/null
```

Or ask: "This branch split from main - is that correct?"

### Step 4: Present Options

**Normal repo and named-branch worktree — present exactly these 4 options:**

```
Implementation complete. What would you like to do?

1. Merge back to <base-branch> locally
2. Push and create a Pull Request
3. Keep the branch as-is (I'll handle it later)
4. Discard this work

Which option?
```

**Detached HEAD — present exactly these 3 options:**

```
Implementation complete. You're on a detached HEAD (externally managed workspace).

1. Push as new branch and create a Pull Request
2. Keep as-is (I'll handle it later)
3. Discard this work

Which option?
```

**Don't add explanation** - keep options concise.

### Step 5: Execute Choice

#### Option 1: Merge Locally

```bash
# Get main repo root for CWD safety
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"

# Merge first — verify success before removing anything
git checkout <base-branch>
git pull
git merge <feature-branch>

# Verify tests on merged result
<test command>

# Only after merge succeeds: cleanup worktree (Step 6), then delete branch
```

Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 6), then delete branch:

```bash
git branch -d <feature-branch>
```

#### Option 2: Push and Create PR

```bash
# Push branch
git push -u origin <feature-branch>
```

**Do NOT clean up worktree** — user needs it alive to iterate on PR feedback.

#### Option 3: Keep As-Is

Report: "Keeping branch <name>. Worktree preserved at <path>."

**Don't cleanup worktree.**

#### Option 4: Discard

**Confirm first:**
```
This will permanently delete:
- Branch <name>
- All commits: <commit-list>
- Worktree at <path>

Type 'discard' to confirm.
```

Wait for exact confirmation.

If confirmed:
```bash
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"
```

Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 6), then force-delete branch:
```bash
git branch -D <feature-branch>
```

### Step 6: Cleanup Workspace

**Only runs for Options 1 and 4.** Options 2 and 3 always preserve the worktree.

```bash
GIT_DIR=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
GIT_COMMON=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
WORKTREE_PATH=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
```

**If `GIT_DIR == GIT_COMMON`:** Normal repo, no worktree to clean up. Done.

**If worktree path is under `.worktrees/` or `worktrees/`:** Superpowers created this worktree — we own cleanup.

```bash
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"
git worktree remove "$WORKTREE_PATH"
git worktree prune  # Self-healing: clean up any stale registrations
```

**Otherwise:** The host environment (harness) owns this workspace. Do NOT remove it. If your platform provides a workspace-exit tool, use it. Otherwise, leave the workspace in place.

## Quick Reference

| Option | Merge | Push | Keep Worktree | Cleanup Branch |
|--------|-------|------|---------------|----------------|
| 1. Merge locally | yes | - | - | yes |
| 2. Create PR | - | yes | yes | - |
| 3. Keep as-is | - | - | yes | - |
| 4. Discard | - | - | - | yes (force) |

## Common Mistakes

**Skipping test verification**
- **Problem:** Merge broken code, create failing PR
- **Fix:** Always verify tests before offering options

**Open-ended questions**
- **Problem:** "What should I do next?" is ambiguous
- **Fix:** Present exactly 4 structured options (or 3 for detached HEAD)

**Cleaning up worktree for Option 2**
- **Problem:** Remove worktree user needs for PR iteration
- **Fix:** Only cleanup for Options 1 and 4

**Deleting branch before removing worktree**
- **Problem:** `git branch -d` fails because worktree still references the branch
- **Fix:** Merge first, remove worktree, then delete branch

**Running git worktree remove from inside the worktree**
- **Problem:** Command fails silently when CWD is inside the worktree being removed
- **Fix:** Always `cd` to main repo root before `git worktree remove`

**Cleaning up harness-owned worktrees**
- **Problem:** Removing a worktree the harness created causes phantom state
- **Fix:** Only clean up worktrees under `.worktrees/` or `worktrees/`

**No confirmation for discard**
- **Problem:** Accidentally delete work
- **Fix:** Require typed "discard" confirmation

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Proceed with failing tests
- Merge without verifying tests on result
- Delete work without confirmation
- Force-push without explicit request
- Remove a worktree before confirming merge success
- Clean up worktrees you didn't create (provenance check)
- Run `git worktree remove` from inside the worktree

**Always:**
- Verify tests before offering options
- Detect environment before presenting menu
- Present exactly 4 options (or 3 for detached HEAD)
- Get typed confirmation for Option 4
- Clean up worktree for Options 1 & 4 only
- `cd` to main repo root before worktree removal
- Run `git worktree prune` after removal

content.text: This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation. Summary: 1. Primary Request and Intent: - Initial: Run `/init` to analyze the Hugo static site codebase (`begbie-hugo`, Rod Begbie's personal/coaching site using the vendored "portio" Hugo theme) and create a `CLAUDE.md` file documenting build commands and architecture. - "Modernize the code in this repo" — initially vague; clarified through brainstorming into a Hugo-template-idiom modernization: replace `.Site.X` template access with Hugo's global `site` function, and raw `.Site.BaseURL` string concatenation with `absURL`, across the vendored theme's layouts. Explicitly deferred SCSS/Bootstrap/JS modernization to "later." - After that work merged: "Can you commit the CLAUDE.md, .claude and .entire folders?" — with explicit care needed around `.entire/`'s session logs/metadata (privacy-sensitive, should not be committed per the tool's own `.gitignore` and per `.claude/settings.json`'s deny rule blocking Claude from even reading `.entire/metadata/**`). - "push it up" — push the CLAUDE.md/tooling-config commit to origin main. - "OK, let's modernise the SASS and JS code. Primary goal is improving the page load performance" — the deferred "later" work, now scoped into 3 phases (CSS trim, JS vanilla rewrite, asset pipeline) per user's explicit choice to decompose and to start with Phase 1 (CSS trim: Bootstrap SCSS trim + Font Awesome → inline SVG icons). - User explicitly chose the MORE aggressive JS option for later ("Replace jQuery + plugins with vanilla JS") when asked about JS scope depth — this is a standing decision to honor when Phase 2 eventually begins. - "Yes." (approving Phase 1 design) — "You have the browsing skill to drive a browser for visual inspection" — explicit instruction to use the Chrome DevTools browsing skill/tool for visual verification rather than relying solely on diffing. - "go" — approved the Phase 1 spec, proceed to implementation plan. - "1" — chose subagent-driven-development execution for the Phase 1 plan. - "Looks good. Squash and merge it" — explicit instruction to squash-merge PR #4 for Phase 1. 2. Key Technical Concepts: - Hugo static site generator (local version 0.163.3 extended; Cloudflare Pages pins 0.157.0 via `wrangler.jsonc`'s `HUGO_VERSION`). - Vendored (not submoduled) "portio" Bootstrap-4.5.2-based Hugo theme under `themes/portio`. - Data-driven, toggleable homepage sections: `data/*.yml` files with an `enable: true|false` flag consumed by `{{ if .enable }}` in matching theme partials. - Hugo template idiom modernization: `.Site.Data` → `hugo.Data` (actually deprecated since Hugo v0.156.0, confirmed via context7 MCP docs query); `.Site.X` → `site.X` (not deprecated, but Hugo's docs recommend the global function); raw `.Site.BaseURL` concatenation → `absURL`/`relURL` pipes (Hugo docs explicitly discourage raw `BaseURL` use). - Subagent-driven development workflow (superpowers skill): task-brief extraction script, review-package diff generation script, implementer/reviewer subagent dispatch pattern, progress ledger at `.superpowers/sdd/progress.md`, DONE/DONE_WITH_CONCERNS/BLOCKED/NEEDS_CONTEXT implementer statuses, per-task spec-compliance + code-quality review, final whole-branch review dispatched on the most capable model (opus). - Brainstorming → writing-plans → subagent-driven-development → finishing-a-development-branch workflow chain (all superpowers skills), each gated by explicit user approval. - Git commit signing via SSH (`gpg.format=ssh`, `user.signingkey=~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`) — commits hang silently if the SSH agent has no loaded identity (`ssh-add -l` → "The agent has no identities"). - `.entire` CLI tool integration via git hooks (commit-msg, post-commit, post-rewrite, prepare-commit-msg, pre-push) and Claude Code hooks (`.claude/settings.json`) — ships its own `.gitignore` excluding `tmp/`, `metadata/`, `logs/`, `settings.local.json`, `redactors/local/`. - Font Awesome icon webfont → inline SVG icon replacement pattern: a Hugo partial with a `dict`-based icon-name-to-SVG-markup lookup, `fill="currentColor"` for CSS-driven coloring, sized via a `.icon` wrapper class in CSS rather than inline width/height, preserving old `fa-*` naming as dict keys for backward compatibility with `config.toml` data and existing template class names. - Bootstrap SCSS modular imports — trimming a monolithic `@import ".../bootstrap"` down to only the specific partials a theme actually uses (functions, variables, mixins, root, reboot, type, grid, forms, buttons, transitions, nav, navbar, breadcrumb, pagination, utilities), verified by checking Sass compiles cleanly (undefined variable/mixin errors reveal missing dependencies). - Chrome DevTools Protocol browser automation via `mcp__plugin_superpowers-chrome_chrome__use_browser` (actions: navigate, scroll, eval, screenshot with `fullpage` option, set_viewport/clear_viewport, click) for visual regression verification — discovered and diagnosed a tool-specific bug where viewport screenshots return blank/white images at non-zero scroll positions, while full-page screenshots capture correctly. - `gh api repos///contents/` with `--jq '.content' | base64 -d` as the method for fetching exact/unaltered file content from GitHub (used to get real Font Awesome SVG path data) — necessary because `curl`/`wget` to raw.githubusercontent.com were actively blocked by a context-mode plugin hook in this environment, and `WebFetch` risked AI-summarization corruption of precise numeric path data. - Building a "focused" review-package diff manually (stat + full diff excluding noisy deleted-vendor-file content, via `git diff -- ':!path/to/dir'`) when the standard `review-package` script's output is bloated by large binary/text vendor file deletions, to avoid wasting reviewer subagent context. 3. Files and Code Sections: - **`/Users/rod/build/begbie-hugo/CLAUDE.md`** (created, then committed in `343700b`): Documents Hugo commands (`hugo server -D`, `hugo`), the vendored-theme architecture, the data-driven section pattern, the `hugo.Data` vs `.Site.Data` history, content types, and Cloudflare Pages deployment via `wrangler.jsonc`. - **`themes/portio/layouts/partials/navbar.html`**: Rewritten in Task 1 of the Hugo-template-modernization plan — replaced `.Site.BaseURL` with `"" | absURL`, removed the `{{ $menu := .Site.Menus.main }}` indirection in favor of `{{ range $index, $element := site.Menus.main }}`, replaced `{{ $.Site.BaseURL }}{{ .URL }}` with `{{ .URL | absURL }}`, and `{{ $.Site.BaseURL }}#contact` with `{{ "#contact" | absURL }}`. This caused a pre-approved whitespace-only output diff (a blank line shifted). - **`themes/portio/layouts/partials/footer.html`, `head.html`, `blogSection.html`, `portfolioSection.html`, `blog/list.html`, `blog/single.html`, `contact/list.html`**: All had `.Site.Params.*`/`.Site.Menus.*`/`.Site.RegularPages`/`.Site.BaseURL` converted to `site.*`/`absURL` equivalents across the Hugo-template-modernization plan's Tasks 1-4. - **`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-30-hugo-template-modernization-design.md`** and **`docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-30-hugo-template-modernization.md`**: Design and plan docs for the first modernization phase, committed as `a9ef817` and `b31cce6` respectively, merged into main as PR #3 (commit `a696b19`). - **`.claude/settings.json`** (committed in `343700b`): Contains Entire CLI hook wiring (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/SessionStart/SessionEnd/Stop/UserPromptSubmit) and a `permissions.deny` rule for `Read(./.entire/metadata/**)`. - **`.entire/settings.json`** and **`.entire/.gitignore`** (committed in `343700b`): `.entire/settings.json` contains `{"enabled": true, "telemetry": true}`. `.entire/.gitignore` contains `tmp/`, `settings.local.json`, `metadata/`, `logs/`, `redactors/local/` — deliberately NOT committed: `.entire/logs/`, `.entire/metadata/` (contains `full.jsonl` session transcripts), `.entire/tmp/`. - **`docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-30-css-modernization-phase1-design.md`** (committed `e7e1735` on branch `chore/css-modernize-phase1`): Design doc covering the exact Bootstrap SCSS partial list to keep/drop and the Font Awesome → inline SVG icon.html partial architecture, with an example (illustrative, not final) dict-based icon lookup snippet. - **`docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-30-css-modernization-phase1.md`** (committed `686a63c`): 3-task implementation plan. content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/rod/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/6.1.0/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer the visual companion just-in-time** — NOT upfront. The first time a question would genuinely be clearer shown than described, offer it then (its own message); on approval its browser tab opens for you. If no visual question ever arises, never offer it. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion (just-in-time):** Do NOT offer it upfront. Wait until a question would genuinely be clearer shown than told — a real mockup / layout / diagram question, not merely a UI *topic*. The first time that happens, offer it then, as its own message:
> "This next part might be easier if I show you — I can put together mockups, diagrams, and comparisons in a browser tab as we go. It's still new and can be token-intensive. Want me to? I'll open it for you."

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Only the offer — no clarifying question, summary, or other content. Wait for the user's response. If they accept, start the server with `--open` so their browser opens to the first screen automatically. If they decline, continue text-only and don't offer again unless they raise it.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

Drop jQuery, Slick, Magnific Popup, TweenMax/GSAP; remove dead plugins (Masonry, imagesLoaded, Waypoint); fix the latent Google Maps bug. User already decided the aggressive option (full vanilla rewrite, not just trim+optimize jQuery delivery) in an earlier session.
I'm using the brainstorming skill to design Phase 2. Let me start by exploring the actual JS files to understand what's really there before proposing anything.

input.description: Check google maps config params

content.text:
Good — I now have a full inventory of what's actually live vs. dead. Before proposing anything, one question with real scope impact.

The `#map` div on the contact page has never worked — `config.toml`'s `APIkey = "YOUR GOOGLE MAP API"` is still the placeholder value, and the Google Maps `<script>` loader is already commented out in `footer.html`. Today this renders as an empty rounded 400px box on the contact page (a real visible defect), and `script.js`'s `initialize()` throws a `ReferenceError` in the console (since `google` is undefined) even though nothing calls it visibly.

questions.question: The contact page's Google Map has never actually worked (no real API key was ever set) and shows as an empty box today. What should Phase 2 do with it?

options.label: Remove entirely (Recommended)
options.description: Delete the #map div, initialize() function, data-lat/long/pin attributes, and [params.map] config. Removes the empty-box defect and the console error, and eliminates the last reason script.js touches window.google.

options.label: Keep dead code, just guard it
options.description: Leave the #map div and initialize() in place but add a typeof google check so it no-ops instead of throwing. Preserves the option to wire up a real API key later, keeps the empty box visible for now.

options.label: Wire up a real API key now
options.description: Get a real Google Maps API key from Rod and make the map actually render. Out of scope for a performance-focused JS rewrite unless Rod wants this fixed properly.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "The contact page's Google Map has never actually worked (no real API key was ever set) and shows as an empty box today. What should Phase 2 do with it?"="Remove entirely (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text: Next: the decorative "blob" background animation.

questions.question: hero.html, aboutSection.html, contact/list.html, blog/list.html, and blog/single.html all have a decorative SVG blob whose path morphs continuously via GSAP TimelineMax (infinite yoyo loop, 4 keyframes, ~3s each). It's purely decorative background flair, not interactive. What should Phase 2 do with it?

options.label: Drop the animation, keep static shape (Recommended)
options.description: Delete the TimelineMax loop entirely; the blob SVG stays in the markup but stops morphing (renders as its first keyframe shape, a static decorative background blob). Removes an always-running JS timer for a subtle effect most visitors don't consciously track — best for perf, matches the 'trim JS' goal.

options.label: Rewrite as vanilla JS morph loop
options.description: Hand-roll a small requestAnimationFrame loop that interpolates between the same 4 path 'd' strings with easing, replicating the current visual effect without GSAP. More code to write and maintain for a background decoration.

options.label: Rewrite as CSS @keyframes animation
options.description: Animate the SVG path's d attribute via CSS @keyframes (supported in current Chrome/Firefox/Edge, partial/no support in older Safari). Zero JS, but degrades to a static shape on unsupported browsers, and CSS d-animation is a newer/less common technique.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "hero.html, aboutSection.html, contact/list.html, blog/list.html, and blog/single.html all have a decorative SVG blob whose path morphs continuously via GSAP TimelineMax (infinite yoyo loop, 4 keyframes, ~3s each). It's purely decorative background flair, not interactive. What should Phase 2 do with it?"="Rewrite as CSS @keyframes animation". You can now continue with these answers in mind.
